title: Blanquette de veau
subtitle: Classic French white braise with cream and egg yolk sauce.
excerpt: Pork shoulder simmered gently in stock with aromatics, then finished in a velouté enriched with cream and egg yolk. The meat stays pale and the sauce coats a spoon. French bourgeois cooking at its most precise. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Blanquette de veau. The dish is one of the grande cuisine classics documented in Auguste Escoffier's Le Guide Culinaire (1903, public domain) and in Prosper Montagné's Larousse Gastronomique (1938). Traditionally made with veal shoulder; pork shoulder is used here as a widely available substitute with comparable technique and texture.

recipe.freezeNotes: The cream and egg yolk sauce does not freeze well. The braise base (without the liaison) freezes for up to two months; add the cream and egg yolk when reheating.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the braise the day before; reheat gently and add the cream liaison just before serving.

glossaryTerms.definition: One of the French mother sauces: a light stock-based sauce thickened with a pale roux. The base for blanquette, suprême and many classical French sauces.
A mixture of egg yolk and cream used to enrich and thicken a sauce at the end of cooking. The pan must not boil after adding a liaison or the egg will scramble.

content.text: Blanquette is a white braise. The meat is not browned; it is blanched in boiling water, rinsed, then simmered gently in stock. The pallor is deliberate. The final sauce, a velouté enriched with cream and egg yolk, should be ivory-white and smooth. Served with rice or new potatoes, it is one of the most satisfying dishes in the French repertoire.

The liaison of cream and egg yolk is added at the very end, off the heat. The pan must not return to a boil once the liaison is in. This is the point where the dish can fail; watch the temperature carefully.

Put {{pork-shoulder}} of pork cubes in a large saucepan. Cover with cold water, bring to the boil, then drain and rinse the meat under cold water. Return to the pan with {{onion}} onion, {{carrot}} carrots, {{celery}} celery, {{garlic}} of whole garlic cloves, {{thyme}} of thyme, {{bay-leaves}} of bay leaves and {{stock-chicken}} of stock. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Bring to a gentle simmer; skim off any foam. Cook on low heat for 1 hour until the meat is tender.
Cook the mushrooms and make the velouté

Remove the meat and strain the cooking stock; discard the aromatics. Add {{mushrooms-button}} of mushrooms to the strained stock and simmer for 5 minutes; remove and set aside. Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter in a saucepan over medium heat. Add {{plain-flour}} of flour and cook, stirring, for 2 minutes. Gradually whisk in 600 ml of the cooking stock until smooth. Simmer for 10 minutes, whisking often, until thick enough to coat the back of a spoon.

Whisk {{double-cream}} of double cream with {{egg-yolks}} egg yolks and the juice of {{lemon}} lemon in a bowl. Remove the sauce from the heat. Slowly whisk the liaison into the hot sauce. Return the meat and mushrooms to the sauce. Season with {{white-pepper}} of white pepper and adjust salt. Reheat very gently — do not boil. Scatter with parsley if using.

items.symptom: Sauce scrambled or curdled after adding liaison
items.cause: Sauce was too hot when the egg and cream were added, or the pan returned to the boil.
items.fix: Add the liaison off the heat, then reheat on the lowest possible setting without boiling. If it has scrambled, push through a fine sieve.

items.cause: Not enough roux, or too much stock used.
items.fix: Use only 600 ml of the cooking stock in the velouté. Simmer the sauce longer before adding the liaison.

items.cause: Overcooked, or pork shoulder cut too small.
items.fix: Pork shoulder can go longer than an hour at very low heat without drying; check at 45 minutes and again at 60.

content.text: Blanquette de veau sits at the centre of French bourgeois cooking: elaborate without being showy, classic without being rigid. It is the kind of dish that takes a Sunday afternoon to make properly and is still worth it. Served with steamed rice or boiled new potatoes, with a cold glass of Chablis, it belongs to the category of meals that are as good for the cook's morale as for the guests'.
